title: Brand Identity and Seasons of Business
summary: Erin led a teaching session covering two core frameworks: brand identity as a personal and intentional practice, and the seasons of business (winter, spring, summer, fall) as a cyclical model for understanding growth and preparation. The session then opened to group discussion where participants shared branding tactics, marketing budgets, consulting business models, and reflections on navigating difficult business seasons.

teachings.concept: Brand Identity as Personal, Not Just Corporate
teachings.explanation: In construction and renovation, brand identity is deeply personal because you are inside people's homes and lives. The way you show up, the decisions you make, the conversations you have, and what you say no to all create your brand. It is not reactive—it is intentional.
teachings.quote: How you show up is it, it's as simple as that. How you show up, pretend there's a camera following you around all day long.

teachings.explanation: When clients hire and trust you, they are not just buying a service—they are purchasing your judgment. How you think through problems, respond and recover, create budgets, and evaluate invoices is what trust is built on.
teachings.quote: When someone's actually going to hire you and trust you and put their hard-earned money into you, they're actually saying, hey, I like the way you judge how to do things.

teachings.concept: Share Who You Are or Your Brand Has No Identity
teachings.explanation: Hard work and great execution mean nothing for your brand if you do not share it. Whether through social media, speaking events, community presence, or simple conversations, you must actively communicate who you are and how you operate.
teachings.quote: If you don't share it, your brand has no identity. So the responsibility we have is to share who we are, share how we think.

teachings.concept: Identity-Based Standards: 'I Am the Type of Person Who...'
teachings.explanation: Brand and character are built through non-negotiable personal standards framed as identity statements. These are not aspirations but fixed behaviors that are not up for debate, and they directly shape how you run your business and what your team observes.
teachings.quote: I am the type of person that does X, and I have no opinion about it because it's the right thing to do—that forms a brand.

teachings.concept: Deliver as the Foundation of Brand
teachings.explanation: The most fundamental element of brand identity is simply delivering on what you promise. Deliver results, deliver information, deliver yes and no—creating outcomes for others is the bedrock of reputation.
teachings.quote: Deliver what you said you would do. Deliver results that we promised. Deliver the information that people need to hear. Deliver a yes, deliver a no, but create the results and deliver for other people.

teachings.concept: Seasons of Business Framework (Winter, Spring, Summer, Fall)
teachings.explanation: Business, projects, personnel, and even individual days follow a seasonal cycle. Winter is preparation and weathering difficulty. Spring brings opportunity because of preparation. Summer is the execution and go-time phase. Fall is analysis—reviewing what worked and studying for the next cycle. This framework applies at every time scale from a single day to an entire business lifecycle.
teachings.quote: Once you capture it, it speeds things up in terms of improvement by like 100x. Like if you literally look back at yesterday, how your yesterday went, and you went through the winter, spring, summer, fall, you know exactly what your moves are today.

teachings.concept: Quiet Reflection as a Strategic Tool
teachings.explanation: Intentional periods of slowing down and listening—particularly during holidays or natural lulls—are where real clarity and strategic direction emerge. Erin attributed key business initiatives including her coaching program to insights gained during Christmas breaks spent writing and reflecting.
teachings.quote: This coaching program is a result of me taking two weeks during Christmas and writing and writing and writing and writing. And I just realized I need to know more great people in this industry.

teachings.concept: Starting Positive to Counter Natural Negativity
teachings.explanation: Humans default toward negative thinking. Intentionally setting a positive, excited mindset at the start of each day creates a foundation that influences every decision and instinct throughout the day, especially during hard seasons.
teachings.quote: When I get up—this is so funny—I'm like, today's going to be awesome. Like today's gonna be a great day. Like I'm so excited to see what happens. And that is not a normal, natural thing to wake up... but immediately that is the first thing I do because I know the day is going to be hard.

actionable_advice: During the two-week holiday break, get quiet and go through a personal reflection exercise—journal, think, and analyze where you are in business so you can share your findings when the group reconvenes in January.
Frame your personal non-negotiables as identity statements ('I am the type of person who...') and write them down so they become the operating standard for yourself and your team.

Share who you are publicly—whether through social media, community events, speaking, or neighborhood gatherings—so that people know how you operate and you attract the clients you actually want to work with.

Before investing heavily in marketing or branding polish, let your brand settle and develop first; do not spend significantly on logo refinement or marketing companies until you have a clear sense of who you are.

If you are a consultant helping builders, keep your name and reputation distinct from the builders you work with—ensure your contract is either with the GC or the homeowner, not both, and make sure the homeowner knows who is responsible for delivery.
Check your insurance coverage carefully before entering a consulting or project management role with another builder, as liability exposure can become a significant and unexpected problem.

When creating branded physical materials (pins, candles, signs, shirts), order in the highest volume you can afford to reduce per-unit cost and maximize distribution across trades, vendors, and clients.
Use Canva to create internal marketing materials like proposals, packets, and booklets rather than outsourcing everything—keep content creation lean.
When onboarding a new project manager, immediately put them into a live project so they learn the systems under real conditions and you can observe how they think before things get critical.
Consider combining your personal and business brand identity under one name so that your personal reputation and your company reputation reinforce each other rather than competing.
client_challenges: Carolyn struggles with putting herself and her brand out there publicly on social media, even though she intellectually understands the importance of visibility.

Julie is partnering with a local remodeler to build out his project management infrastructure as a consultant, and is trying to define her scope, her client (builder vs. homeowner), and how to structure her business model going forward.

Bennett is in a rapid growth phase and is questioning how much of his business direction has been intentional versus pulled out of him by client demand, and how to start being more deliberate about what he says yes to.

Renee has experienced a prolonged difficult season with challenges across multiple areas—closing jobs, staying on budget, a client dispute, and even a nine-month battle with Google Business verification—and is wrestling with distinguishing productive creative thinking from self-doubt and desperation.
Janine is looking for recommendations on external marketing companies or social media consultants who have experience in the construction or design-build industry.

Brittany resolved a difficult situation from a previous session by deciding to repaint a client's house at significant cost to herself, working out a payment plan with her painters, which reduced anxiety but caused financial pain.
Multiple participants are navigating how to handle the holiday construction slowdown—balancing giving trades and staff time off while maintaining progress on active projects.
Participants are uncertain how much to spend on social media management, with budgets ranging widely, and want to understand what is reasonable given changing AI and search landscape.
notable_quotes: On brand identity: 'How you show up is it, it's as simple as that. Pretend there's a camera following you around all day long.'
On what clients actually buy: 'They're purchasing my judgment. That's what trust is about.'
On visibility: 'If you don't share it, your brand has no identity. You need to tell people who you are.'
On non-negotiables: 'I am the type of person that does X, and I have no opinion about it because it's the right thing to do—that forms a brand.'
On the seasons framework: 'Once you capture it, it speeds things up in terms of improvement by like 100x.'
On resilience, shared by participant Ryan in chat: 'Don't wish it was easier. Wish you were better.'
